title: Count number of connected components using DSU.
description: Count number of connected components using DSU.

examples.explanation: Step 1: Initialize visited=[False,False,False,False,False], count=0. Step 2: Node 0 not visited → count=1, DFS from 0: mark 0 visited, neighbors [1] → DFS 1: mark visited, neighbors [0,2] → DFS 2: mark visited, neighbors [1]. Step 3: Node 3 not visited → count=2, DFS from 3: mark 3 visited, neighbors [4] → DFS 4: mark visited, neighbors [3]. Step 4: All nodes visited, return count=2.

Step 1: visited=[False,False,False,False], count=0. Step 2: Node 0 not visited → count=1, DFS from 0: mark 0 visited, neighbors [1] → DFS 1: mark visited, neighbors [0,2] → DFS 2: mark visited, neighbors [1,3] → DFS 3: mark visited, neighbors [2]. Step 3: All nodes visited, return count=1.

approaches.name: Brute Force DFS/BFS O(V+E)
approaches.summary: Use DFS or BFS to traverse the graph, marking visited nodes to count connected components. This approach works on any graph representation but doesn't use DSU.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Connected Components in Undirected Graph using DFS/BFS
ladders.desc: Given an undirected graph, count the number of connected components using DFS or BFS traversal.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires counting how many separate groups of connected nodes exist in an undirected graph. The intuition is that each connected component is a maximal set of nodes where you can travel between any two nodes via edges. We can solve this by performing graph traversal (DFS or BFS) from each unvisited node, marking all reachable nodes as visited, and incrementing our count for each new traversal start. This works because each traversal will explore exactly one connected component completely. The algorithm: 1) Initialize visited array with False for all nodes. 2) Initialize component count to 0. 3) For each node, if not visited: increment count, perform DFS/BFS from that node to mark all connected nodes as visited. 4) Return count. Time complexity is O(V+E) where V is vertices and E is edges since we visit each node and edge once. Space complexity is O(V) for visited array and recursion stack/queue. This connects to the parent problem by providing a complete solution. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle disconnected graphs properly, not marking nodes as visited before processing neighbors (causing infinite loops), and incorrect graph representation. Real code pattern: def count_components(n, edges): visited = [False]*n; count = 0; for i in range(n): if not visited[i]: count += 1; dfs(i, visited, adj_list); return count.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize visited=[False,False,False,False,False], count=0. Step 2: Node 0 not visited → count=1, DFS from 0: mark 0 visited, neighbors [1] → DFS 1: mark visited, neighbors [0,2] → DFS 2: mark visited, neighbors [1]. Step 3: Node 3 not visited → count=2, DFS from 3: mark 3 visited, neighbors [4] → DFS 4: mark visited, neighbors [3]. Step 4: All nodes visited, return count=2.

Step 1: visited=[False,False,False,False], count=0. Step 2: Node 0 not visited → count=1, DFS from 0: mark 0 visited, neighbors [1] → DFS 1: mark visited, neighbors [0,2] → DFS 2: mark visited, neighbors [1,3] → DFS 3: mark visited, neighbors [2]. Step 3: All nodes visited, return count=1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Traversal to Mark Connected Nodes
ladders.desc: Implement DFS to traverse and mark all nodes in a connected component as visited.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine performs depth-first search from a starting node to explore all nodes reachable via edges, marking them as visited. The intuition is that DFS recursively explores as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking, ensuring we visit every node in the connected component. The algorithm: 1) Mark current node as visited. 2) For each neighbor of current node: if neighbor not visited, recursively call DFS on neighbor. This ensures we traverse all edges in the component. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(V_c+E_c) where V_c and E_c are vertices and edges in that component, but overall O(V+E) when called from main function. Space complexity is O(V) for recursion stack. This feeds into L0 by being the core traversal mechanism. Common mistakes: infinite recursion if visited check is after recursive call, not building adjacency list correctly. Real code pattern: def dfs(node, visited, adj): visited[node]=True; for neighbor in adj[node]: if not visited[neighbor]: dfs(neighbor, visited, adj).

examples.trace: Step 1: Mark node 0 visited → visited=[True,False,False]. Step 2: Neighbors of 0: [1] → neighbor 1 not visited → DFS(1). Step 3: Mark node 1 visited → visited=[True,True,False]. Step 4: Neighbors of 1: [0,2] → neighbor 0 visited, neighbor 2 not visited → DFS(2). Step 5: Mark node 2 visited → visited=[True,True,True]. Step 6: Neighbors of 2: [1] → neighbor 1 visited, return.

Step 1: Mark node 3 visited → visited=[True,True,True,True,False]. Step 2: Neighbors of 3: [4] → neighbor 4 not visited → DFS(4). Step 3: Mark node 4 visited → visited=[True,True,True,True,True]. Step 4: Neighbors of 4: [3] → neighbor 3 visited, return.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterating Over Nodes and Counting Components
ladders.desc: Loop through all nodes, starting a new traversal for each unvisited node to count components.
ladders.explanation: The central insight is that each connected component corresponds to exactly one starting point in our iteration where the node is unvisited. By iterating through all nodes and initiating DFS/BFS only when we encounter an unvisited node, we ensure we count each component once. The intuition: if a node is already visited, it belongs to a component we've already counted; if unvisited, it's the start of a new component. Algorithm: 1) Initialize count=0. 2) For each node i from 0 to n-1: if not visited[i], increment count and call traversal from i. 3) Return count. Time complexity is O(V) for the loop plus O(V+E) for traversals, overall O(V+E). Space is O(V). This connects to L1 by using the traversal function. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in loop range, not resetting count correctly. Real code pattern: count=0; for i in range(n): if not visited[i]: count+=1; dfs(i, visited, adj).

examples.trace: Step 1: count=0. Step 2: i=0, not visited → count=1, DFS from 0 marks all nodes visited. Step 3: i=1, visited → skip. Step 4: i=2, visited → skip. Step 5: Return count=1.

Step 1: count=0. Step 2: i=0, not visited → count=1, DFS from 0 marks nodes 0,1 visited. Step 3: i=1, visited → skip. Step 4: i=2, not visited → count=2, DFS from 2 marks nodes 2,3 visited. Step 5: i=3, visited → skip. Step 6: Return count=2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Constructing Adjacency List from Edge List
ladders.desc: Convert a list of edges into an adjacency list representation for efficient graph traversal.
ladders.explanation: An adjacency list is a data structure where each node has a list of its neighboring nodes, enabling efficient traversal. The intuition is that for undirected graphs, each edge connects two nodes bidirectionally, so we add each node to the other's list. Algorithm: 1) Initialize adj as a list of empty lists of size n. 2) For each edge [u,v] in edges: append v to adj[u] and u to adj[v]. This ensures O(1) average access to neighbors. Time complexity is O(E) to process all edges. Space is O(V+E) to store the lists. This connects to L2 by providing the graph representation needed for traversal. Common mistakes: forgetting to add both directions for undirected graphs, index out of bounds if n is incorrect. Real code pattern: adj=[[] for _ in range(n)]; for u,v in edges: adj[u].append(v); adj[v].append(u).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Marking a Node as Visited
ladders.desc: Implement the simple operation of marking a node as visited in a boolean array.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves setting a boolean value to True in an array to indicate that a node has been processed. The intuition is that we need to track which nodes we've already explored to avoid reprocessing and infinite loops. Algorithm: 1) Initialize visited array of size n with all False. 2) When visiting a node i, set visited[i]=True. This operation is O(1) time and O(1) space per node. It connects to L3 by being used in graph traversal to manage state. Common mistakes: using wrong index, not initializing array properly. Real code pattern: visited = [False]*n; visited[node] = True.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize visited=[False,False,False]. Step 2: Mark node 1: visited[1]=True → visited=[False,True,False].

Step 1: visited=[False,False,False,False,False]. Step 2: Mark node 0: visited[0]=True → visited=[True,False,False,False,False]. Step 3: Mark node 3: visited[3]=True → visited=[True,False,False,True,False].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Graphs and Nodes
ladders.desc: Learn the basic concepts of graphs, nodes, and edges as prerequisites for graph algorithms.
ladders.explanation: A graph is a collection of nodes (vertices) connected by edges. In an undirected graph, edges have no direction, meaning if node A is connected to node B, then B is connected to A. Nodes represent entities, and edges represent relationships. For example, in a social network, nodes are people, and edges are friendships. The intuition is that graphs model pairwise relationships, and we can traverse them to explore connections. Key terms: vertex (node), edge (link), adjacency (neighboring nodes). This foundational concept connects to L4 by explaining what we're marking as visited. Common mistakes: confusing directed and undirected graphs. Real code pattern: Represent nodes as integers 0 to n-1, edges as pairs of integers.

examples.trace: Step 1: Identify nodes: 0, 1, 2. Step 2: Edge 0-1 connects nodes 0 and 1. Step 3: Edge 1-2 connects nodes 1 and 2. Step 4: This forms a chain: 0 connected to 1, 1 connected to 2.

Step 1: Nodes: 0,1,2,3. Step 2: Edge 0-1 connects nodes 0 and 1 into one pair. Step 3: Edge 2-3 connects nodes 2 and 3 into another pair. Step 4: These are two separate groups with no connections between them.

approaches.name: Basic DSU with Path Compression O(α(n))
approaches.summary: Implement Disjoint Set Union with union by rank/size and path compression. Count components by tracking the number of distinct roots after processing all edges.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Connected Components Using DSU
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of counting connected components in an undirected graph using DSU with union by size and path compression.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires counting the number of connected components in an undirected graph using Disjoint Set Union (DSU). The intuition is that each node starts as its own component, and when we process edges, we union the nodes connected by each edge, merging their components. After processing all edges, each distinct root in the DSU represents one connected component. The key insight is that DSU efficiently tracks connectivity with near-constant time operations using union by size/rank and path compression. The algorithm initializes a DSU with n nodes, processes each edge to union the connected nodes, then counts distinct roots. Time complexity is O(n + m * α(n)) where α is the inverse Ackermann function (effectively constant), and space is O(n). This feeds directly from L1 by using the optimized union and find operations to build the component structure. Common mistakes include not initializing parent/rank arrays correctly, forgetting path compression, or miscounting components by not checking all nodes' roots. Real code pattern: Initialize parent[i]=i, size[i]=1; for each edge (u,v): union(u,v); count = sum(1 for i in range(n) if find(i)==i).

examples.trace: Initialize DSU for 5 nodes: parent=[0,1,2,3,4], size=[1,1,1,1,1]. Process edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, union by size (size[0]=1, size[1]=1, attach 1 to 0): parent[1]=0, size[0]=2. Process edge [1,2]: find(1)=find(1)=0 (path compression: parent[1]=0), find(2)=2, union (size[0]=2, size[2]=1, attach 2 to 0): parent[2]=0, size[0]=3. Process edge [3,4]: find(3)=3, find(4)=4, union (size[3]=1, size[4]=1, attach 4 to 3): parent[4]=3, size[3]=2. After all edges: parent=[0,0,0,3,3]. Find roots: find(0)=0, find(1)=0, find(2)=0, find(3)=3, find(4)=3. Distinct roots: 0 and 3 → count=2.

Initialize DSU for 4 nodes: parent=[0,1,2,3], size=[1,1,1,1]. Process edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, union (size[0]=1, size[1]=1, attach 1 to 0): parent[1]=0, size[0]=2. Process edge [2,3]: find(2)=2, find(3)=3, union (size[2]=1, size[3]=1, attach 3 to 2): parent[3]=2, size[2]=2. Process edge [1,2]: find(1)=find(1)=0, find(2)=2, union (size[0]=2, size[2]=2, attach 2 to 0): parent[2]=0, size[0]=4. After all edges: parent=[0,0,0,2] (note: 3's parent is 2, which points to 0). Find roots: find(0)=0, find(1)=0, find(2)=find(2)=0 (path compression: parent[2]=0), find(3)=find(3)=find(2)=0. Distinct root: 0 → count=1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DSU Union and Find with Optimizations
ladders.desc: Implement the core DSU operations: find with path compression and union by size.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the two fundamental DSU operations: find (to determine the root of a node) and union (to merge two sets). The intuition is that we maintain a parent array where each node points to its parent, and the root points to itself. Find recursively follows parent pointers until reaching the root, and applies path compression by setting all visited nodes to point directly to the root, flattening the tree for future efficiency. Union merges two sets by attaching the smaller tree under the larger one (union by size), keeping the tree shallow. This ensures near-constant time per operation via the inverse Ackermann function. The algorithm: find(x): if parent[x]!=x: parent[x]=find(parent[x]); return parent[x]. union(x,y): rootX=find(x), rootY=find(y); if rootX!=rootY: if size[rootX]<size[rootY]: swap rootX and rootY; parent[rootY]=rootX; size[rootX]+=size[rootY]. Time per operation is O(α(n)), space O(n). This feeds into L0 by providing the efficient connectivity operations needed to process edges. Common mistakes include infinite recursion in find without base case, not updating size correctly, or not handling equal sizes properly. Real code pattern: parent=list(range(n)), size=[1]*n; def find(x): while parent[x]!=x: parent[x]=parent[parent[x]]; x=parent[x]; return x; def union(x,y): rx,ry=find(x),find(y); if rx!=ry: if size[rx]<size[ry]: rx,ry=ry,rx; parent[ry]=rx; size[rx]+=size[ry].

examples.input: Initialize n=4, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(1,2)
examples.trace: Start: parent=[0,1,2,3], size=[1,1,1,1]. union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, size[0]=1, size[1]=1, attach 1 to 0: parent[1]=0, size[0]=2. union(2,3): find(2)=2, find(3)=3, size[2]=1, size[3]=1, attach 3 to 2: parent[3]=2, size[2]=2. union(1,2): find(1)=find(1)=0, find(2)=2, size[0]=2, size[2]=2, attach 2 to 0: parent[2]=0, size[0]=4. Final: parent=[0,0,0,2], size=[4,1,2,1]. Note: 3's parent is 2, which points to 0.

examples.input: Initialize n=5, operations: union(0,1), union(1,2), union(3,4), find(2) with path compression
examples.trace: Start: parent=[0,1,2,3,4], size=[1,1,1,1,1]. union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, attach 1 to 0: parent[1]=0, size[0]=2. union(1,2): find(1)=find(1)=0, find(2)=2, attach 2 to 0: parent[2]=0, size[0]=3. union(3,4): find(3)=3, find(4)=4, attach 4 to 3: parent[4]=3, size[3]=2. find(2): parent[2]=0, parent[0]=0 → return 0, and apply path compression: set parent[2]=0 directly. Final root of 2 is 0.

testCases.input: n=5, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(0,2), find(4)
n=6, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(1,3), find(2)
n=4, operations: union(0,1), union(1,2), union(2,3), find(3)
n=5, operations: union(0,1), union(1,2), union(3,4), find(0)
n=7, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(0,2), union(4,6), find(3)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Merging Sets via Union by Size
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the union operation that merges two disjoint sets by attaching smaller tree to larger.
ladders.explanation: This core logic focuses on the union operation in DSU, specifically using union by size to keep trees balanced. The intuition is that when merging two sets, we attach the root of the smaller tree under the root of the larger tree. This minimizes tree height, ensuring that find operations remain efficient. By tracking the size of each tree (number of nodes in the set), we can decide which tree is smaller. The algorithm: given two nodes x and y, find their roots rootX and rootY. If they are different (i.e., in different sets), compare sizes: if size[rootX] < size[rootY], swap them so rootX is the larger root. Then set parent[rootY] = rootX and update size[rootX] += size[rootY]. This ensures the combined tree has depth O(log n) in worst case, but with path compression it becomes amortized O(α(n)). Time per union is O(α(n)) due to find calls, space O(1) extra. This feeds into L1 by providing the merging strategy that works with find. Common mistakes include not checking if roots are already the same (leading to cycles), incorrect size updates, or not swapping roots properly. Real code pattern: def union(x,y): rx,ry=find(x),find(y); if rx!=ry: if size[rx]<size[ry]: rx,ry=ry,rx; parent[ry]=rx; size[rx]+=size[ry].

examples.input: Start with 4 isolated nodes, sizes [1,1,1,1]. Perform union(0,1), union(2,3), union(1,2).
examples.trace: Initial: parent=[0,1,2,3], size=[1,1,1,1]. union(0,1): roots 0 and 1, sizes both 1, attach 1 to 0: parent[1]=0, size[0]=2. union(2,3): roots 2 and 3, sizes both 1, attach 3 to 2: parent[3]=2, size[2]=2. union(1,2): find(1)=0, find(2)=2, size[0]=2, size[2]=2, equal so attach 2 to 0 (or 0 to 2, but we pick 0 as larger after no swap): parent[2]=0, size[0]=4. Final: parent=[0,0,0,2], size=[4,1,2,1].

examples.input: Start with 5 nodes, sizes [1,1,1,1,1]. Perform union(0,1), union(0,2), union(3,4), union(0,3).
examples.output: Final sizes and parent of node 4
examples.trace: Initial: parent=[0,1,2,3,4], size=[1,1,1,1,1]. union(0,1): attach 1 to 0: parent[1]=0, size[0]=2. union(0,2): find(0)=0, find(2)=2, size[0]=2, size[2]=1, attach 2 to 0: parent[2]=0, size[0]=3. union(3,4): attach 4 to 3: parent[4]=3, size[3]=2. union(0,3): find(0)=0, find(3)=3, size[0]=3, size[3]=2, attach 3 to 0: parent[3]=0, size[0]=5. Final: parent=[0,0,0,0,3], size=[5,1,1,2,1]. Parent of node 4 is 3, which points to 0.

testCases.input: n=3, initial sizes [1,1,1], union(0,1), union(1,2), size of root 0
n=4, initial sizes [1,1,1,1], union(0,1), union(2,3), union(0,2), size of root 0
n=5, initial sizes [1,1,1,1,1], union(0,1), union(2,3), size of root 2
n=2, initial sizes [1,1], union(0,1), size of root 0
n=6, initial sizes [1,1,1,1,1,1], union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(0,2), size of root 0
n=3, initial sizes [1,1,1], union(0,1), size of root 1
n=4, initial sizes [1,1,1,1], union(0,1), union(0,2), union(0,3), size of root 0
n=5, initial sizes [1,1,1,1,1], union(0,1), union(1,2), union(3,4), size of root 3
n=1, initial sizes [1], union(0,0), size of root 0
n=7, initial sizes [1,1,1,1,1,1,1], union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(0,2), union(4,6), size of root 4

ladders.title: Building Block — Path Compression in Find Operation
ladders.desc: Implement the find operation with path compression to flatten the tree structure.
ladders.explanation: This building block implements the find operation in DSU with path compression, a key optimization that flattens the tree structure during root searches. The intuition is that when we find the root of a node, we recursively update all nodes along the path to point directly to the root, reducing the height of the tree for future operations. This ensures that subsequent finds become nearly constant time. The algorithm uses recursion or iteration: recursively call find on the parent until reaching the root (where parent[x] == x), then on the way back, set parent[x] to the root. Alternatively, an iterative approach uses a while loop to compress the path. Time complexity is O(α(n)) amortized due to compression, space O(1) extra per call. This feeds into L2 by making find efficient, which is crucial for union operations. Common mistakes include infinite recursion without base case, not updating parent pointers correctly, or using a non-recursive method without compression. Real code pattern: def find(x): if parent[x] != x: parent[x] = find(parent[x]); return parent[x]. Or iterative: while parent[x] != x: parent[x] = parent[parent[x]]; x = parent[x]; return x.

examples.input: Initial parent array: [0,0,1,2] for nodes 0-3. Perform find(3) with path compression.
examples.trace: Start: parent=[0,0,1,2]. find(3): parent[3]=2 != 3, so call find(2). find(2): parent[2]=1 != 2, call find(1). find(1): parent[1]=0 != 1, call find(0). find(0): parent[0]=0, return 0. Back to find(1): set parent[1]=0, return 0. Back to find(2): set parent[2]=0, return 0. Back to find(3): set parent[3]=0, return 0. Final parent=[0,0,0,0], root=0.

examples.input: Initial parent array: [0,1,2,3,4] for nodes 0-4. Perform find(4) (no compression needed), then union(0,1), union(1,2), find(2) with compression.
examples.trace: Start: parent=[0,1,2,3,4]. find(4): parent[4]=4, return 4, no change. union(0,1): set parent[1]=0. union(1,2): find(1)=0, find(2)=2, set parent[2]=0. Now parent=[0,0,0,3,4]. find(2): parent[2]=0 != 2, call find(0): parent[0]=0, return 0. Set parent[2]=0, return 0. Final parent=[0,0,0,3,4] (2's parent updated to 0 directly).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Initializing and Querying Parent Array
ladders.desc: Set up the parent array for DSU and perform basic find without compression.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves initializing the DSU data structure and performing a simple find operation without optimizations. The intuition is that we represent each set as a tree, where each node has a parent pointer, and the root is the representative of the set. Initially, each node is its own parent, forming n singleton sets. The find operation traverses parent pointers until reaching a node that is its own parent (the root). This is the foundation for more advanced optimizations. The algorithm: initialize parent array of size n with parent[i] = i. For find(x), while parent[x] != x, set x = parent[x]; return x. Time complexity is O(h) where h is tree height (could be O(n) worst-case without optimizations), space O(n). This feeds into L3 by providing the basic structure that path compression improves. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in initialization, infinite loops in find if parent points to itself incorrectly, or not handling negative indices. Real code pattern: parent = list(range(n)); def find(x): while parent[x] != x: x = parent[x]; return x.

examples.input: n=5, initialize parent array, then perform find(0), find(4).
examples.trace: Initialize parent = [0,1,2,3,4]. find(0): parent[0]=0, return 0. find(4): parent[4]=4, return 4. No changes to parent.

examples.input: n=4, initialize parent array, set parent[1]=0, parent[2]=1, parent[3]=2. Perform find(3).
examples.trace: Start with parent = [0,0,1,2] after manual sets. find(3): parent[3]=2 != 3, so x=2. parent[2]=1 != 2, so x=1. parent[1]=0 != 1, so x=0. parent[0]=0, so return 0. Root is 0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Sets and Representatives
ladders.desc: Understand the idea of partitioning elements into disjoint sets with a representative element.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces the core idea behind DSU: partitioning a collection of elements into disjoint sets, where each set has a representative element (often called the root or parent). The intuition is that we group related items together, and each group has a single identifier that we can use to check if two items are in the same group. For example, in a social network, each friend group might have a leader; if two people share the same leader, they are in the same group. Initially, each element is in its own set, so it is its own representative. We can then merge sets by picking one representative to represent the combined set. This is the simplest prerequisite for DSU, focusing on the abstract concept without implementation details. Time and space are not analyzed here as it's conceptual. This feeds into L4 by motivating the need for a parent array to track representatives. Common mistakes include confusing sets with lists or not understanding disjointness. Real code pattern: conceptually, we might use a dictionary or array to map each element to its representative, but here we just understand the idea.

examples.input: Elements: {A,B,C,D}. Initially, each in own set. Merge sets containing A and B, then merge sets containing C and D.
examples.trace: Start: sets {A}, {B}, {C}, {D}. Representatives: A for {A}, B for {B}, C for {C}, D for {D}. Merge A and B: choose A as representative, new set {A,B} with rep A. Merge C and D: choose C as representative, new set {C,D} with rep C. Final: sets {A,B} rep A, {C,D} rep C.

examples.input: Elements: {1,2,3,4,5}. Initially own sets. Merge sets with 1 and 2, then merge sets with 3 and 4, then merge sets with 1 and 3.
examples.trace: Start: sets {1}, {2}, {3}, {4}, {5}. Representatives: 1,2,3,4,5. Merge 1 and 2: choose 1 as rep, set {1,2} rep 1. Merge 3 and 4: choose 3 as rep, set {3,4} rep 3. Merge 1 and 3: choose 1 as rep (or 3, but pick 1), combined set {1,2,3,4} rep 1. Final: sets {1,2,3,4} rep 1, {5} rep 5.

testCases.input: Elements {A,B,C}, all in same set initially
Elements {P,Q,R,S}, merge P and Q, merge R and S
Elements {1,2,3,4}, merge 1 and 2, merge 3 and 4, merge 1 and 3
Elements {1,2,3,4,5}, merge 1 and 5, merge 2 and 4, merge 1 and 2
Elements {A,B,C,D}, merge A and B, merge C and D, merge B and C
Elements {1,2}, merge 1 and 2, then merge 1 and 2 again

approaches.name: Iterative DSU without Recursion O(α(n))
approaches.summary: Implement DSU using iterative methods for find operations instead of recursion. This avoids recursion depth limits and has the same theoretical complexity.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Connected Components with Iterative DSU
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to count connected components in an undirected graph using iterative DSU.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires counting the number of connected components in an undirected graph using Disjoint Set Union (DSU) with iterative path compression. The intuition is that DSU efficiently tracks connectivity by grouping nodes into sets, where each set represents a connected component. Initially, each node is its own component. As edges are processed, we union the sets of connected nodes. The number of distinct sets (roots) after processing all edges gives the component count. The iterative find operation avoids recursion depth issues while maintaining near-constant time complexity via path compression. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize DSU arrays: parent[i] = i, rank[i] = 0. 2. For each edge (u, v), call union(u, v). 3. Union uses iterative find to get roots, then merges sets by rank to optimize. 4. After all unions, count unique roots using iterative find on all nodes. Time: O(n + m * α(n)) where n = nodes, m = edges, α(n) is inverse Ackermann (effectively constant). Space: O(n). This feeds directly from L1's union operation. Common mistakes: not using path compression (slower), forgetting to initialize all nodes, or miscounting roots. Real code pattern: class DSU with __init__, find, union methods, then iterate edges and count roots.

examples.trace: Initialize: parent=[0,1,2,3,4], rank=[0,0,0,0,0]. Edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, union: rank[0]=0, rank[1]=0, set parent[0]=1, parent=[1,1,2,3,4]. Edge [1,2]: find(1)=1 (path: 1->1), find(2)=2, union: rank[1]=0, rank[2]=0, set parent[1]=2, parent=[1,2,2,3,4]. Edge [3,4]: find(3)=3, find(4)=4, union: rank[3]=0, rank[4]=0, set parent[3]=4, parent=[1,2,2,4,4]. Count roots: find(0): 0->1->2, return 2; find(1): 1->2, return 2; find(2): 2, return 2; find(3): 3->4, return 4; find(4): 4, return 4. Unique roots: {2,4}, count=2.

Initialize: parent=[0,1,2,3], rank=[0,0,0,0]. Edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, union: set parent[0]=1, parent=[1,1,2,3], rank[1]=1. Edge [2,3]: find(2)=2, find(3)=3, union: set parent[2]=3, parent=[1,1,3,3], rank[3]=1. Edge [1,2]: find(1)=1, find(2)=2->3, union: rank[1]=1, rank[3]=1, set parent[3]=1, parent=[1,1,3,1], rank[1]=2. Count roots: find(0): 0->1, return 1; find(1): 1, return 1; find(2): 2->3->1, return 1; find(3): 3->1, return 1. Unique roots: {1}, count=1.

testCases.input: n=100, edges=[[i,i+1] for i in range(99)]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Union Operation with Iterative Find
ladders.desc: Implement the union function that merges two sets using iterative find and rank optimization.
ladders.explanation: The union operation is crucial for DSU as it combines two sets into one, representing connectivity between nodes. The intuition is to connect the roots of two elements, ensuring the tree remains balanced to optimize future operations. Using iterative find avoids recursion and prevents stack overflow. Step-by-step: 1. Call find(x) and find(y) to get roots rootX and rootY. 2. If rootX == rootY, they are already connected, do nothing. 3. Else, use rank to decide which root becomes parent: if rank[rootX] < rank[rootY], set parent[rootX] = rootY; if rank[rootX] > rank[rootY], set parent[rootY] = rootX; if equal, choose one (e.g., rootY as parent) and increment its rank. Time: O(α(n)) per union due to find and constant-time merge. Space: O(1). This feeds into L0 by processing edges. Common mistakes: not checking if roots are equal (causes cycles), incorrect rank updates, or not using iterative find. Real code pattern: def union(x, y): rootX = find(x); rootY = find(y); if rootX != rootY: if rank[rootX] < rank[rootY]: parent[rootX] = rootY; elif rank[rootX] > rank[rootY]: parent[rootY] = rootX; else: parent[rootY] = rootX; rank[rootX] += 1.

examples.trace: Start: parent=[0,1,2], rank=[0,0,0]. find(0)=0, find(1)=1. rootX=0, rootY=1, not equal. rank[0]=0, rank[1]=0, equal. Choose rootY=1 as parent: set parent[0]=1. Increment rank[1] to 1. Result: parent=[1,1,2], rank=[0,1,0].

Start: parent=[1,1,2,3], rank=[0,1,0,0]. find(2)=2, find(3)=3. rootX=2, rootY=3, not equal. rank[2]=0, rank[3]=0, equal. Choose rootY=3 as parent: set parent[2]=3. Increment rank[3] to 1. Result: parent=[1,1,3,3], rank=[0,1,0,1].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterative Find with Path Compression
ladders.desc: Implement the find function iteratively to locate the root of an element and compress the path.
ladders.explanation: The find operation is the heart of DSU, determining which set an element belongs to by finding its root. Iterative implementation avoids recursion limits and is efficient. The intuition is to traverse up the parent chain until reaching the root, then compress the path by updating all visited nodes to point directly to the root, flattening the tree for future speed. Step-by-step: 1. Start with node x. 2. While parent[x] != x, move up: x = parent[x] (this finds the root). 3. Store the root. 4. Second pass: reset x to original, traverse again, setting each parent to the root. Alternatively, a one-pass method can be used by tracking nodes. Time: O(α(n)) amortized due to compression. Space: O(1). This feeds into L1's union. Common mistakes: not compressing the path (leads to O(n) time), infinite loops if parent pointers are incorrect, or using recursion in deep trees. Real code pattern: def find(x): root = x; while parent[root] != root: root = parent[root]; while x != root: next_node = parent[x]; parent[x] = root; x = next_node; return root.

examples.trace: Start: x=0. First pass: parent[0]=1 != 0, set x=1; parent[1]=2 != 1, set x=2; parent[2]=2 == 2, root=2. Second pass: reset x=0, parent[0]=1 != root, set parent[0]=2, x=1; parent[1]=2 == root, set parent[1]=2, x=2; x=root, stop. Return 2. Final parent=[2,2,2,3].

Start: x=3. First pass: parent[3]=2 != 3, set x=2; parent[2]=1 != 2, set x=1; parent[1]=0 != 1, set x=0; parent[0]=0 == 0, root=0. Second pass: reset x=3, parent[3]=2 != root, set parent[3]=0, x=2; parent[2]=1 != root, set parent[2]=0, x=1; parent[1]=0 == root, set parent[1]=0, x=0; x=root, stop. Return 0. Final parent=[0,0,0,0].

ladders.title: Building Block — Parent and Rank Arrays Initialization
ladders.desc: Set up the DSU data structures: parent array (each node points to itself) and rank array (all zeros).
ladders.explanation: Initialization is the foundation of DSU, creating the data structures that track set membership and tree height. The intuition is that initially, each node is its own connected component, so it points to itself as parent. Rank tracks the depth of the tree to optimize unions. Step-by-step: 1. Create parent array of size n. 2. For i from 0 to n-1, set parent[i] = i. 3. Create rank array of size n, initialized to 0. Time: O(n). Space: O(n). This feeds into L2's find operation. Common mistakes: not initializing all nodes, using wrong array sizes, or setting rank incorrectly (should start at 0). Real code pattern: parent = [i for i in range(n)]; rank = [0] * n.

examples.trace: For n=3: create parent array of length 3. Set parent[0]=0, parent[1]=1, parent[2]=2. Create rank array of length 3, set all to 0. Result: parent=[0,1,2], rank=[0,0,0].

For n=5: create parent array of length 5. Set parent[0]=0, parent[1]=1, parent[2]=2, parent[3]=3, parent[4]=4. Create rank array of length 5, set all to 0. Result: parent=[0,1,2,3,4], rank=[0,0,0,0,0].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Access and Update
ladders.desc: Perform fundamental array operations: read an element and update an element.
ladders.explanation: Array operations are essential for DSU as parent and rank are stored in arrays. The intuition is that arrays provide O(1) access and update, enabling efficient set tracking. Step-by-step: 1. Reading: given array arr and index i, access arr[i] to get the value. 2. Updating: set arr[i] = new_value. Time: O(1) per operation. Space: O(1). This feeds into L3's initialization and later operations. Common mistakes: out-of-bounds access, not using correct indices, or misunderstanding zero-based indexing. Real code pattern: val = arr[i]; arr[i] = new_val.

examples.input: arr=[10,20,30], read index 1, update index 2 to 40
examples.trace: Start: arr=[10,20,30]. Read index 1: arr[1] = 20. Update index 2: set arr[2] = 40. Result: read value is 20, arr becomes [10,20,40].

examples.input: arr=[5,6,7,8], read index 0, update index 3 to 9
examples.trace: Start: arr=[5,6,7,8]. Read index 0: arr[0] = 5. Update index 3: set arr[3] = 9. Result: read value is 5, arr becomes [5,6,7,9].

testCases.input: arr=[1], read index 0, update index 0 to 2
arr=[0,0], read index 1, update index 0 to 1
arr=[3,4,5], read index 2, update index 1 to 6
arr=[10,20,30,40], read index 3, update index 2 to 50
arr=[], read index 0, update index 0 to 1
arr=[7,8,9], read index 0, update index 2 to 10
arr=[1,2,3,4,5], read index 4, update index 0 to 0
arr=[100], read index 0, update index 0 to 200
arr=[6,7], read index 1, update index 1 to 8
arr=[0,1,0,1], read index 2, update index 3 to 2

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indices
ladders.desc: Understand arrays as ordered collections and how to use indices to access elements.
ladders.explanation: Arrays are fundamental data structures that store elements in contiguous memory locations, accessible via indices. The intuition is that an array is like a list of boxes numbered starting from 0, where each box holds a value. This allows efficient storage and retrieval. Step-by-step: 1. Define an array: e.g., arr = [3, 7, 2]. 2. Access elements: arr[0] gives 3 (first element), arr[1] gives 7, arr[2] gives 2. 3. Indices range from 0 to length-1. Time: O(1) for access. Space: O(n) for storage. This feeds into L4's operations. Common mistakes: using 1-based indexing (should be 0-based), accessing out-of-bounds indices, or confusing indices with values. Real code pattern: arr = [value1, value2, ...]; element = arr[index].

examples.trace: Array has elements: index 0 = 5, index 1 = 10, index 2 = 15. Access index 1: return the value at position 1, which is 10.
Array has elements: index 0 = 0, index 1 = 1, index 2 = 2, index 3 = 3. Access index 3: return the value at position 3, which is 3.
